protocol_definition.name: Debate
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents answer independently, critique competing answers, and Writer synthesizes.
protocol_definition.visibility: Initial answers are private; critique round exposes competing answers.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after the configured critique rounds and Writer output.

final_call.expected_action: Synthesize the strongest factually corrected answer. Resolve disagreements against the task constraints and return only the self-contained requested deliverable.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Independently produce a complete solution. You cannot see other answers yet.
intermediate_messages.content:
1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that scores documents based on term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, matching exact character sequences after tokenization. It has no inherent understanding of word meaning, synonyms, or word order beyond the presence/absence of terms. BM25 scoring itself is not positional exact-phrase matching; phrase handling requires a separate post-filter or index structure (e.g., positional index) to verify term adjacency and order.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a trained neural model (e.g., a dual-encoder). Relevance is computed as the cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query embedding and document embeddings. It captures semantic similarity, synonymy, and paraphrasing, but may struggle with exact term matching, rare identifiers, and precise phrase constraints unless the encoder was specifically fine-tuned for those patterns.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical | Dense Embedding | Hybrid |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|--------|
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap; no semantics | Semantic similarity; fuzzy token matching | Combines exact and semantic signals |
| Synonyms | No native support; requires query expansion | Captures synonyms if present in training data | Both signals available |
| Exact terms & identifiers | Strong; matches literal strings (e.g., error codes, product codes) | Weak unless fine-tuned; may map to similar but incorrect concepts | BM25 anchors exact matches; dense adds recall |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires positional index or post-filter; BM25 score alone does not enforce phrase order | No native phrase enforcement; embedding loses word order | Phrase enforcement must be layered on top of either method |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Inverted index; lightweight; no GPU | Vector index (e.g., FAISS); requires embedding model; GPU recommended for encoding | Both indexes; fusion logic; higher operational complexity |
| Updates | Add/remove documents by updating inverted index; immediate | Re-encode new documents; re-index vectors; may require incremental index rebuild | Both update paths must be maintained |
| Latency | Low; sparse dot product or term-at-a-time scoring | Moderate; nearest-neighbor search over dense vectors | Sum of both retrieval latencies plus fusion overhead |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch; misses paraphrases; over-prioritizes rare terms; no semantic generalization | Misses rare codes/identifiers; false semantic matches; embedding drift over time | Fusion weight tuning; one weak signal can dominate; increased complexity |

4. Eight-query routing table

| Query ID | Important Signal | Preferred Route | Rationale | Failure Risk |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|-----------|--------------|
| Q1. ERR-1042 reset token expired | Exact error code “ERR-1042”; token “reset token” | BM25-first | BM25 reliably matches the literal code; dense may confuse “ERR-1042” with other error patterns | If FAQ uses variant “error 1042” without “ERR-”, BM25 may miss; mitigation: normalize error codes in index |
| Q2. I forgot my password and cannot get back into my account | Paraphrase of “password reset”/“account recovery” | Dense-first | High semantic variation; no fixed identifier; dense captures “forgot password” ≈ “reset password” | Dense may retrieve unrelated account issues; mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “password” term to anchor |
| Q3. What does product code AX-17 mean? | Exact product code “AX-17” | BM25-first | Literal string match critical; dense may not distinguish “AX-17” from “AX-18” or similar codes | If code appears only in body text without “product code” label, BM25 may rank low; mitigation: boost title/exact-match fields |
| Q4. Please send my billing receipt again | Action-oriented; “billing receipt” is a specific concept | Hybrid | “billing receipt” is a compound concept; BM25 matches the terms; dense captures “send again” ≈ “resend” | Dense may retrieve general billing FAQs; mitigation: use BM25 phrase proximity on “billing receipt” |
| Q5. Disable two-factor authentication for a contractor | Specific action “disable 2FA”; entity “contractor” | Hybrid | “two-factor authentication” has synonyms (2FA, MFA); BM25 matches literal; dense handles “disable” ≈ “turn off” | Dense may conflate “contractor” with “employee”; mitigation: entity filtering if metadata available |
| Q6. I am not receiving notification emails | Symptom description; no fixed code | Dense-first | High paraphrase variability (“not getting emails”, “emails not arriving”); dense generalizes well | Dense may retrieve unrelated email configuration issues; mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “notification” term |
| Q7. Search for the exact phrase "data export completed" | Explicit exact-phrase requirement | BM25-first with phrase post-filter | Only BM25 with positional index can enforce exact phrase adjacency; dense cannot guarantee word order | If phrase is split across fields, positional check may fail; mitigation: concatenate title+body for phrase index |
| Q8. How can I move my workspace information to another account? | Semantic concept “migrate workspace data” | Dense-first | No fixed identifier; “move” ≈ “transfer” ≈ “migrate”; “workspace information” ≈ “workspace data” | Dense may retrieve “share workspace” instead of “transfer”; mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “workspace” and “account” |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document corpus, a simple two-stage policy suffices:
- Stage 1 – Route: Classify query as BM25-first if it contains a known pattern (error code regex like `[A-Z]+-\d+`, product code, quoted phrase). Otherwise, route as Dense-first.
- Stage 2 – Fusion: Always retrieve top-K from both methods (K=20 each). Combine scores using a weighted linear combination: `score = α * norm(BM25) + (1-α) * norm(dense_cosine)`. Set α=0.7 for BM25-first queries, α=0.3 for Dense-first queries. Normalize scores via min-max scaling per result set.
- Phrase enforcement: For queries containing quoted phrases, apply a positional post-filter to BM25 results before fusion; documents failing the phrase check are removed or demoted.
- Tie-breaking: Prefer the document with higher BM25 score when fusion scores are within 1% to preserve exact-match precision.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations
- Risk 1: Vocabulary mismatch on error codes. If FAQ uses “Error 1042” but query says “ERR-1042”, BM25 may miss. Mitigation: Normalize error codes in both index and query (e.g., strip “ERR-” prefix, case-fold) before tokenization.
- Risk 2: Dense embedding drift after FAQ updates. Adding 50 new documents changes the vector space; previously good matches may degrade. Mitigation: Re-encode and re-index the full corpus on each update; for 500 docs this is cheap and fast.
- Risk 3: Fusion weight mis-calibration. A fixed α may over-prioritize one signal for edge-case queries. Mitigation: Implement a fallback: if top fused result score < threshold (e.g., 0.2 after normalization), re-rank using reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) as a parameter-free alternative and return the better list.
- Risk 4: Exact-phrase false positives. Positional post-filter may reject a document where the phrase spans a field boundary (title ends with “data export”, body starts with “completed”). Mitigation: Index a concatenated full-text field for phrase search.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
- Create a judgment set: For each of the 8 query cases, manually annotate all 500 documents as relevant (1) or not (0). Include at least 3 paraphrased variants per query to test robustness (24 total query-document pairs to judge exhaustively).
- Metrics: Compute Precision@5, Recall@5, and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) for each route (BM25-only, Dense-only, Hybrid) on the annotated set.
- Error analysis: For each query, inspect false positives and false negatives. Categorize failures as: vocabulary mismatch, semantic drift, phrase violation, or fusion weight error.
- Ablation: Run hybrid with α ∈ {0.0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0} and compare MRR to validate routing weights.
- Phrase integrity check: For Q7, manually verify that all top-5 results contain the exact contiguous phrase “data export completed”.
- No automated metrics requiring external models; all judgments are human-annotated.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
Deploy a hybrid system with BM25 (using an inverted index with positional information) and a dense retriever (using a lightweight dual-encoder fine-tuned on in-domain FAQ pairs if available, otherwise a general sentence encoder). Use the routing/fusion policy described in Section 5. For 500 documents, infrastructure overhead is minimal: both indexes fit in memory, and re-indexing on updates is fast. This approach preserves exact matching for codes and phrases while adding semantic recall for paraphrased queries, directly addressing the failure modes observed in the eight query cases.

1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that scores documents based on term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, matching exact character sequences (after tokenization). It has no inherent understanding of word meaning, synonyms, or word order beyond the presence/absence of terms. BM25 scoring itself is not positional exact-phrase matching; phrase handling requires a separate post-filter or index structure (e.g., positional indexes for span queries).

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a neural model (e.g., a sentence transformer). Relevance is computed via vector similarity (typically cosine or dot product). The model captures semantic similarity, paraphrasing, and synonyms through its training data. It does not rely on exact lexical overlap and has no native mechanism for exact term matching, exact identifiers, or strict phrase matching unless those patterns were seen during training and are reflected in the embedding space.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical | Dense Embedding | Hybrid |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|--------|
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap; no semantics | Semantic similarity; fuzzy matching | Combines exact and semantic signals |
| Synonyms | Misses unless query and doc share tokens | Captures trained synonyms and paraphrases | Covers both via fusion |
| Exact terms & identifiers | Strong; matches literal strings (e.g., error codes, product IDs) | Weak; may map to similar concepts, losing precision | Strong if BM25 component weighted for exact matches |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires positional index/span query (not BM25 score alone) | No native phrase matching; proximity lost in embedding | Requires explicit phrase filter or BM25 phrase extension |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Inverted index; lightweight; no GPU | Vector index (e.g., FAISS); GPU recommended for encoding; higher storage | Both indexes; fusion logic; moderate complexity |
| Updates | Incremental index update; immediate | Re-encode and re-index; model may need fine-tuning for new terms | Update both indexes; fusion logic unchanged |
| Latency | Very low; mature optimizations | Low with approximate nearest neighbor (ANN); encoding latency added | Sum of both; can parallelize |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch; misses paraphrases; no semantic generalization | Misses rare codes/identifiers; false positives from semantic drift; phrase confusion | Fusion weight tuning; complexity; can inherit failures of both if not calibrated |

4. Eight-query routing table

| Query ID | Important Signal | Preferred Route | Rationale | Failure Risk |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|-----------|--------------|
| Q1. ERR-1042 reset token expired | Exact error code "ERR-1042" | BM25-first | Error code is a precise lexical token; dense may confuse with similar codes or generic errors. BM25 ensures exact match. | If FAQ uses variant like "Error 1042" without hyphen, BM25 may miss; mitigation: tokenization normalization. |
| Q2. I forgot my password and cannot get back into my account | Paraphrase, synonyms ("forgot password", "reset password", "can't log in") | Dense-first | High semantic variation; users phrase this many ways. Dense captures intent across synonyms. | May retrieve unrelated account issues; mitigation: fuse with BM25 on key terms like "password" to anchor. |
| Q3. What does product code AX-17 mean? | Exact identifier "AX-17" | BM25-first | Product code is a literal string; dense may not have seen it in training and could map to wrong product. BM25 guarantees exact match. | If FAQ spells out "AX 17" without hyphen, BM25 tokenization may split; mitigation: character n-gram indexing or normalized field. |
| Q4. Please send my billing receipt again | Action-oriented paraphrase ("send receipt", "resend invoice", "billing receipt") | Dense-first | Multiple ways to request receipt; dense handles "send again" = "resend". | May retrieve generic billing FAQs not about resending; mitigation: boost BM25 on "receipt" + "billing". |
| Q5. Disable two-factor authentication for a contractor | Specific action + entity type ("disable 2FA", "contractor", "turn off MFA") | Hybrid | "Two-factor authentication" has synonyms (2FA, MFA, multi-factor); "contractor" is a specific user role. Dense captures synonyms; BM25 anchors "contractor" and "disable". | Dense may miss "contractor" if training data lacks role-specific language; BM25 ensures presence. |
| Q6. I am not receiving notification emails | Symptom description ("not getting emails", "email notifications not working", "missing alerts") | Dense-first | High paraphrase variability; dense captures "not receiving" ≈ "missing" ≈ "not getting". | May retrieve general email setup FAQs; mitigation: BM25 boost on "notification" + "email". |
| Q7. Search for the exact phrase "data export completed" | Exact phrase match | BM25-first with phrase filter | Explicit request for exact phrase; BM25 scoring alone insufficient—requires positional phrase query. Dense cannot guarantee phrase contiguity. | If phrase appears with intervening words, phrase filter fails; mitigation: allow slop parameter or fallback to dense. |
| Q8. How can I move my workspace information to another account? | Semantic intent ("move workspace", "transfer data", "migrate account") | Dense-first | Complex intent with multiple paraphrases; dense captures "move" ≈ "transfer" ≈ "migrate", "workspace information" ≈ "workspace data". | May retrieve account creation FAQs; mitigation: BM25 anchor on "workspace" and "account". |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage approach balances precision and recall without heavy infrastructure:
- **Indexing**: Build a BM25 inverted index with positional data (for phrase queries) and a dense vector index (e.g., flat index or small ANN index; 500 docs trivial for brute-force).
- **Routing logic**:
  - If query contains a known pattern (error code regex like `[A-Z]+-\d+`, product code, quoted phrase), route to BM25-first with phrase/term boosting.
  - Else if query length > 5 tokens and no rare identifiers, route to Dense-first.
  - For ambiguous or mixed-signal queries (e.g., contains both a code and natural language), use Hybrid.
- **Fusion (Hybrid)**: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60. Combine BM25 and dense result lists; no score normalization needed. For phrase queries, apply a positional phrase filter to BM25 candidates before fusion.
- **Fallback**: If top result score (dense similarity or BM25) is below a threshold, expand to hybrid fusion to catch misses.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations
- **Risk 1: Identifier mismatch in dense retrieval.** Dense models may map rare codes (e.g., "ERR-1042") to semantically similar but incorrect documents (e.g., "ERR-1043").  
  *Mitigation*: Route identifier-heavy queries to BM25-first; maintain a regex pattern list for known codes; if BM25 returns exact match, boost to top.
- **Risk 2: Phrase matching failure in dense retrieval.** Dense embeddings lose word order; "data export completed" may match "export completed data" or "data import completed".  
  *Mitigation*: For quoted phrases, use BM25 positional phrase query; if no exact phrase found, fall back to dense with a warning or lower confidence.
- **Risk 3: Fusion weight staleness.** As FAQ content evolves, new terms (e.g., new product codes) may not be well-represented in dense embeddings, and fusion weights may become suboptimal.  
  *Mitigation*: Periodically re-encode documents when new terms are added; monitor click-through rates per route; adjust RRF k or routing rules based on feedback.
- **Risk 4: Short query ambiguity.** Queries like "AX-17" could be a product code or a part number; BM25 may return multiple documents with that string in different contexts.  
  *Mitigation*: Use document structure (title vs. body field weighting) to prioritize FAQ titles; if ambiguity persists, present top-3 results.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
- **Test collection**: Create 30–50 representative queries covering the eight query types (exact code, paraphrase, phrase, mixed). Include edge cases: misspellings, partial codes, very short queries.
- **Relevance judgments**: For each query, manually label each of the 500 documents as relevant (1) or not (0). Since corpus is small, exhaustive judging is feasible.
- **Metrics**: Compute Precision@1, Recall@5, MRR, and NDCG@5 for each route (BM25-only, Dense-only, Hybrid) and the routing policy overall.
- **Error analysis**: For each failure (relevant doc not in top 5), categorize reason: vocabulary mismatch, semantic drift, phrase order error, identifier confusion, fusion weight issue.
- **Routing accuracy**: Measure whether the routing decision (BM25-first, Dense-first, Hybrid) would have selected the best-performing method for that query. Compute routing precision.
- **Latency simulation**: Measure end-to-end response time for each route on representative hardware; ensure hybrid does not exceed latency budget.
- **Iterate**: Adjust routing rules and fusion parameters based on error categories; re-evaluate.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document FAQ system, implement a lightweight hybrid system with rule-based routing. BM25 with positional phrase support handles exact codes, identifiers, and quoted phrases. Dense retrieval (using a small, general-purpose sentence encoder) handles paraphrases and natural language queries. Use RRF fusion for ambiguous queries. This balances the precision of lexical matching for known-item queries with the recall of semantic search for exploratory or variably phrased questions, without requiring GPU infrastructure at query time (dense encoding of 500 docs is a one-time offline cost; query encoding is lightweight). The routing policy keeps latency low by avoiding unnecessary dense searches for clear lexical queries. Regular qualitative evaluation on a curated test set will guide tuning as the FAQ corpus evolves.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that scores documents based on term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, counting exact token matches in the document. The scoring formula is:
score(D,Q) = Σ IDF(qi) * (f(qi,D) * (k1+1)) / (f(qi,D) + k1*(1-b+b*(|D|/avgdl)))
where f(qi,D) is term frequency, |D| is document length, avgdl is average document length, and k1, b are tunable parameters. BM25 has no inherent understanding of word order, semantics, or synonyms—it only matches exact tokens after text preprocessing (tokenization, lowercasing, stemming). Positional exact-phrase matching is a separate post-retrieval or query-time operation that checks whether terms appear consecutively in the specified order; BM25 scoring alone does not enforce or reward adjacency.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a neural model (e.g., a sentence transformer). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query embedding and document embeddings in a high-dimensional vector space. The model is typically trained on paired query-document data to capture semantic similarity, paraphrasing, and topical relatedness. Retrieval is performed via approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search over pre-computed document embeddings. Dense retrieval captures synonyms and conceptual matches but may struggle with exact term matching, rare identifiers, and precise phrase constraints unless the training data specifically reinforces those patterns.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical | Dense Embedding |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap; no semantics | Semantic similarity; fuzzy token matching |
| Synonyms | No (unless query expanded) | Yes, if captured in training |
| Exact terms & identifiers | Strong (exact string match) | Weak; may map to similar but wrong terms |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires separate positional filter; BM25 score alone ignores order | No inherent phrase awareness; embedding collapses word order |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Inverted index; minimal compute; no GPU | Vector index (ANN); GPU for encoding; higher storage |
| Updates | Fast: re-index changed documents only | Requires re-encoding and index update; slower |
| Latency | Low (inverted index lookup) | Moderate (ANN search + encoding latency) |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch; misses paraphrases; no semantic generalization | Misses rare codes/IDs; false semantic matches; phrase blindness |

4. Eight-query routing table

| Query ID | Important Signal | Preferred Route | Rationale | Failure Risk |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|-----------|---------------|
| Q1 | Exact error code "ERR-1042" | BM25-first | Identifier requires exact lexical match; dense may confuse with similar codes | If document uses variant like "ERR 1042" without hyphen, BM25 may miss; mitigation: normalize tokenization |
| Q2 | Paraphrase of "forgot password" | Dense-first | Multiple natural language variations; no exact term dependency | May retrieve unrelated account issues; mitigation: combine with BM25 on "password" for precision |
| Q3 | Exact product code "AX-17" | BM25-first | Alphanumeric code demands exact match; dense embeddings unlikely to distinguish from AX-16, AX-18 | Code absent from FAQ but described indirectly; mitigation: fallback to dense for descriptions |
| Q4 | Exact phrase "billing receipt" | Hybrid | "billing receipt" is a specific compound; BM25 catches the terms, dense catches "send again" / "resend" variations | Dense may retrieve general billing docs; mitigation: boost BM25 phrase matches |
| Q5 | Specific action "disable two-factor authentication" | Hybrid | "two-factor authentication" has synonyms (2FA, MFA); dense handles variations, BM25 anchors on "disable" + "contractor" | Dense may retrieve enabling 2FA docs; mitigation: negative query signals or metadata filter |
| Q6 | Paraphrase "not receiving notification emails" | Dense-first | Highly variable phrasing (not getting emails, missing alerts); no fixed terminology | May retrieve email setup docs instead of troubleshooting; mitigation: BM25 re-rank on "notification" + "email" |
| Q7 | Exact phrase "data export completed" | BM25-first | Explicit phrase search requires positional matching; dense cannot enforce word order | Phrase split across fields; mitigation: concatenate title+body for phrase search |
| Q8 | Paraphrase "move workspace information to another account" | Dense-first | Conceptual query with many surface forms (transfer, migrate, copy workspace); no fixed terms | May retrieve account creation docs; mitigation: BM25 filter on "workspace" AND "account" |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage approach balances latency and accuracy:
- Stage 1 (Routing): Classify query as lexical-heavy or semantic-heavy using simple heuristics:
  - If query contains alphanumeric codes (regex: \b[A-Z]+-\d+\b), quoted phrases, or is short (<4 words) with rare terms → BM25-first.
  - If query is a natural language question (>5 words), contains common verbs (forgot, cannot, how can I), or has no identifiers → Dense-first.
  - Otherwise → Hybrid (both paths).
- Stage 2 (Fusion): For hybrid queries, retrieve top-20 from BM25 and top-20 from dense. Combine scores using reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) with k=60: score(d) = Σ 1/(k+rank_i(d)). Return top-10 fused results.
- For routed queries, retrieve top-20 from primary method, then optionally re-rank top-10 using the secondary method's scores as a tiebreaker or confidence check.
- Exact-phrase queries: apply a post-retrieval positional filter on BM25 candidate documents before fusion.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations
- Risk 1: Identifier confusion in dense retrieval. Dense embeddings may map "ERR-1042" close to "ERR-1043" or "ERR-1042a" due to substring similarity in training.
  - Mitigation: Pre-process queries to detect error codes; force BM25-first route and boost exact string matches. Add a character n-gram index for fuzzy code matching as fallback.
- Risk 2: Phrase blindness in dense retrieval. Query "data export completed" may retrieve documents about "data import failed" because embeddings capture topic but not word order.
  - Mitigation: For quoted queries, apply positional phrase filter on BM25 results and suppress dense results that lack the consecutive token sequence. Use shingle-based indexing for common phrases.
- Risk 3: Vocabulary gap in BM25 for paraphrased queries. "Move workspace information" vs. document "Transfer your workspace data" yields zero BM25 overlap.
  - Mitigation: Dense-first routing catches these; for hybrid, ensure dense results are not overruled by BM25 zeros. Optionally, apply lightweight query expansion on BM25 side using FAQ-specific synonyms (move→transfer, information→data).
- Risk 4: Fusion dilution. RRF may elevate a document ranked 15th in both lists over one ranked 1st in BM25 and 50th in dense, losing a highly precise lexical match.
  - Mitigation: Weighted RRF where BM25 rank gets higher weight for code-heavy queries, or apply a minimum BM25 score threshold before fusion.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
- Create a relevance judgment set: For each of the 500 FAQ documents, generate 2-3 natural language queries that should retrieve that document as the top answer. Include variations: exact code queries, paraphrased questions, phrase queries, and mixed queries. Target ~50 judged query-document pairs covering all eight query archetypes.
- Metrics: Measure Recall@5, MRR, and NDCG@5 for each retrieval method (BM25-only, Dense-only, Hybrid) on the judgment set. No automated metric substitutes for human review.
- Qualitative analysis: For each query, manually inspect top-5 results from each method. Categorize failures:
  - Lexical gap (BM25 missed due to vocabulary)
  - Semantic drift (Dense retrieved topically related but incorrect doc)
  - Phrase violation (Dense ignored word order)
  - Identifier mismatch (Dense confused similar codes)
  - Fusion error (Hybrid ranked wrong doc higher)
- A/B comparison: For the eight fixed queries, have two annotators independently rate top-3 results as "Perfect", "Acceptable", "Irrelevant". Compute agreement and identify systematic weaknesses.
- Edge case testing: Deliberately inject queries with typos (ERR-1042 → ERR-104), near-miss codes, and extremely short queries to stress-test routing logic.
- Iterate on routing heuristics and fusion weights based on failure patterns observed.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document FAQ system, implement a lightweight hybrid with rule-based routing. BM25 alone will fail on paraphrased natural language questions (common in FAQ). Dense alone will fail on error codes, product IDs, and exact-phrase searches. A full hybrid with RRF fusion adds minimal latency for this corpus size and covers both failure modes. The routing heuristics prevent dense retrieval from mishandling identifier queries and prevent BM25 from missing semantic variations. Given the small corpus, both inverted and vector indexes can be held in memory, keeping latency under 50ms. No external vendors or GPU clusters are required; open-source dense models (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) and BM25 libraries (e.g., rank_bm25) suffice. Prioritize building the judgment set early to validate routing rules before deployment.

BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that scores documents based on term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, matching exact character sequences after tokenization. It has no inherent understanding of word meaning, synonyms, or word order beyond the presence/absence of terms. BM25 scoring itself is not positional exact-phrase matching; phrase handling requires a separate post-filter or index structure (e.g., positional index) to verify term adjacency and order.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a trained neural model (e.g., a dual-encoder). Relevance is computed as the cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query embedding and document embeddings. It captures semantic similarity, synonymy, and paraphrasing, but may struggle with exact term matching, rare identifiers, and precise phrase constraints unless the encoder was specifically fine-tuned for those patterns.

| Exact terms & identifiers | Strong; matches literal strings (e.g., error codes, product codes) | Weak unless fine-tuned; may map to similar but incorrect concepts | BM25 anchors exact matches; dense adds recall |

1. BM25 definition
All three proposals correctly define BM25 as a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function based on TF, IDF, and document length normalization. They accurately note that BM25 scoring itself is not positional exact-phrase matching; phrase handling requires a separate post-filter or index structure. Proposal 3 adds the explicit BM25 formula, which is accurate but unnecessary for the task. No errors in this section.

2. Dense retrieval definition
All proposals correctly describe dense retrieval as encoding queries and documents into fixed-length embeddings using a neural model, with relevance computed via cosine similarity or dot product. They accurately note the lack of native exact term or phrase matching. No errors.

3. Technical comparison table
All proposals provide accurate comparisons. However, Proposal 1’s table includes a “Hybrid” column, which is not strictly required but adds value. Proposal 2 and 3 limit the table to BM25 vs. Dense, which is acceptable. No factual errors in the comparisons. The constraint “BM25 scoring itself is not the same as positional exact-phrase matching” is correctly reflected in all tables.

4. Eight-query routing table
All proposals correctly assign routes and provide rationales. However, there are inconsistencies and potential weaknesses:

- Q4 (“Please send my billing receipt again”): Proposal 1 routes to Hybrid, Proposal 2 to Dense-first, Proposal 3 to Hybrid. The query contains a specific compound concept (“billing receipt”) and an action paraphrase (“send again”). A hybrid approach is safer because BM25 anchors the exact compound while dense captures the paraphrase. Proposal 2’s Dense-first route risks retrieving generic billing FAQs without the resend intent. Proposal 1 and 3 are more robust.
- Q5 (“Disable two-factor authentication for a contractor”): All route to Hybrid, which is appropriate given the mix of synonyms (2FA/MFA) and a specific entity (“contractor”).
- Q7 (“Search for the exact phrase ‘data export completed’”): All correctly route to BM25-first with phrase post-filter, explicitly noting that BM25 scoring alone is insufficient. This satisfies the task’s precision requirement.
- Failure risks: Proposals identify valid risks (e.g., variant error codes, phrase split across fields) and propose reasonable mitigations. No unsupported claims.

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
- Proposal 1: Two-stage with regex-based routing, weighted linear combination, and phrase post-filter. The α values (0.7/0.3) are arbitrary but reasonable for a starting point. The tie-breaking rule is sensible.
- Proposal 2: Uses RRF (k=60) instead of score normalization, which is parameter-free and often robust. Includes a fallback threshold. The routing logic is slightly more complex (query length, rare identifiers).
- Proposal 3: Similar to Proposal 2 but adds a re-ranking step for routed queries and a minimum BM25 threshold. The RRF approach is well-justified for small corpora.
- Critique: Proposal 1’s linear combination requires score normalization, which can be brittle with small result sets. RRF in Proposals 2 and 3 avoids this. Proposal 3’s weighted RRF for code-heavy queries is a good mitigation for fusion dilution. All policies are lightweight and appropriate for 500 documents. No factual errors.

6. Failure risks and mitigations
All proposals list at least three risks with mitigations. Common risks: vocabulary mismatch on identifiers, dense embedding drift, phrase matching failure, fusion weight issues. Mitigations are practical (normalization, re-indexing, fallback strategies). Proposal 3 adds a risk about fusion dilution, which is a valid concern with RRF. No unsupported claims.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
All proposals describe creating a judgment set, computing standard metrics (Precision@5, Recall@5, MRR), and performing error analysis. Proposal 1 suggests annotating all 500 documents for 24 query variants, which is feasible but labor-intensive. Proposal 2 suggests 30–50 queries with exhaustive judging, which is more practical. Proposal 3 suggests generating queries from documents, which risks bias but is acceptable for a small corpus. All plans are qualitative, avoid external tools, and include manual inspection. No performance numbers are invented.

8. Final recommendation
All recommend a hybrid system with BM25 (positional index) and dense retrieval, using lightweight fusion. They correctly note that 500 documents fit in memory and that re-indexing is fast. Proposal 3 mentions specific open-source tools (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, rank_bm25), which is acceptable as an example, not a vendor endorsement. No unsupported claims.

Overall Assessment:
- No proposal violates hard constraints or invents performance numbers.
- Proposal 1’s linear fusion is slightly less robust than RRF for small, varied result sets.
- Proposal 2’s routing logic for Q4 (Dense-first) is riskier than the Hybrid routes in Proposals 1 and 3.
- Proposal 3 provides the most detailed failure analysis (e.g., fusion dilution) and practical mitigations (weighted RRF, character n-gram index).
- All proposals correctly emphasize that BM25 scoring alone does not handle exact phrases, satisfying the task’s precision requirement.

Recommendation: Proposal 3 is the strongest because it uses RRF (avoiding score normalization pitfalls), addresses fusion dilution explicitly, and provides concrete, low-cost implementation suggestions. However, its Q4 route should be Hybrid, not Dense-first, to anchor the compound term “billing receipt.” Proposal 1’s Q4 Hybrid route is preferable. A merged approach taking Proposal 3’s fusion policy and Proposal 1’s Q4 routing would be optimal.

1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that scores documents based on term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, counting exact token matches in the document. The scoring formula is:
score(D,Q) = Σ IDF(qi) * (f(qi,D) * (k1+1)) / (f(qi,D) + k1*(1-b+b*(|D|/avgdl)))
where f(qi,D) is term frequency, |D| is document length, avgdl is average document length, and k1, b are tunable parameters. BM25 has no inherent understanding of word order, semantics, or synonyms—it only matches exact tokens after text preprocessing (tokenization, lowercasing, stemming). Positional exact-phrase matching is a separate post-retrieval or query-time operation that checks whether terms appear consecutively in the specified order; BM25 scoring alone does not enforce or reward adjacency.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a neural model (e.g., a sentence transformer). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query embedding and document embeddings in a high-dimensional vector space. The model is typically trained on paired query-document data to capture semantic similarity, paraphrasing, and topical relatedness. Retrieval is performed via approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search over pre-computed document embeddings. Dense retrieval captures synonyms and conceptual matches but may struggle with exact term matching, rare identifiers, and precise phrase constraints unless the training data specifically reinforces those patterns.

1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that scores documents based on term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, counting exact token matches in the document. The scoring formula is:
score(D,Q) = Σ IDF(qi) * (f(qi,D) * (k1+1)) / (f(qi,D) + k1*(1-b+b*(|D|/avgdl)))
where f(qi,D) is term frequency, |D| is document length, avgdl is average document length, and k1, b are tunable parameters. BM25 has no inherent understanding of word order, semantics, or synonyms—it only matches exact tokens after text preprocessing (tokenization, lowercasing, stemming). Positional exact-phrase matching is a separate post-retrieval or query-time operation that checks whether terms appear consecutively in the specified order; BM25 scoring alone does not enforce or reward adjacency.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a neural model (e.g., a sentence transformer). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query embedding and document embeddings in a high-dimensional vector space. The model is typically trained on paired query-document data to capture semantic similarity, paraphrasing, and topical relatedness. Retrieval is performed via approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search over pre-computed document embeddings. Dense retrieval captures synonyms and conceptual matches but may struggle with exact term matching, rare identifiers, and precise phrase constraints unless the training data specifically reinforces those patterns.

1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that scores documents based on term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, counting exact token matches in the document. The scoring formula is:
score(D,Q) = Σ IDF(qi) * (f(qi,D) * (k1+1)) / (f(qi,D) + k1*(1-b+b*(|D|/avgdl)))
where f(qi,D) is term frequency, |D| is document length, avgdl is average document length, and k1, b are tunable parameters. BM25 has no inherent understanding of word order, semantics, or synonyms—it only matches exact tokens after text preprocessing (tokenization, lowercasing, stemming). Positional exact-phrase matching is a separate post-retrieval or query-time operation that checks whether terms appear consecutively in the specified order; BM25 scoring alone does not enforce or reward adjacency.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a neural model (e.g., a sentence transformer). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query embedding and document embeddings in a high-dimensional vector space. The model is typically trained on paired query-document data to capture semantic similarity, paraphrasing, and topical relatedness. Retrieval is performed via approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search over pre-computed document embeddings. Dense retrieval captures synonyms and conceptual matches but may struggle with exact term matching, rare identifiers, and precise phrase constraints unless the training data specifically reinforces those patterns.

It does not rely on exact lexical overlap and has no native mechanism for exact term matching, exact identifiers, or strict phrase matching unless those patterns were seen during training and are reflected in the embedding space.

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage approach balances precision and recall without heavy infrastructure:
- **Indexing**: Build a BM25 inverted index with positional data (for phrase queries) and a dense vector index (e.g., flat index or small ANN index; 500 docs trivial for brute-force).
- **Routing logic**:
  - If query contains a known pattern (error code regex like `[A-Z]+-\d+`, product code, quoted phrase), route to BM25-first with phrase/term boosting.
  - Else if query length > 5 tokens and no rare identifiers, route to Dense-first.
  - For ambiguous or mixed-signal queries (e.g., contains both a code and natural language), use Hybrid.
- **Fusion (Hybrid)**: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60. Combine BM25 and dense result lists; no score normalization needed. For phrase queries, apply a positional phrase filter to BM25 candidates before fusion.
- **Fallback**: If top result score (dense similarity or BM25) is below a threshold, expand to hybrid fusion to catch misses.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations
- **Risk 1: Identifier mismatch in dense retrieval.** Dense models may map rare codes (e.g., "ERR-1042") to semantically similar but incorrect documents (e.g., "ERR-1043").  
  *Mitigation*: Route identifier-heavy queries to BM25-first; maintain a regex pattern list for known codes; if BM25 returns exact match, boost to top.
- **Risk 2: Phrase matching failure in dense retrieval.** Dense embeddings lose word order; "data export completed" may match "export completed data" or "data import completed".  
  *Mitigation*: For quoted phrases, use BM25 positional phrase query; if no exact phrase found, fall back to dense with a warning or lower confidence.
- **Risk 3: Fusion weight staleness.** As FAQ content evolves, new terms (e.g., new product codes) may not be well-represented in dense embeddings, and fusion weights may become suboptimal.  
  *Mitigation*: Periodically re-encode documents when new terms are added; monitor click-through rates per route; adjust RRF k or routing rules based on feedback.
- **Risk 4: Short query ambiguity.** Queries like "AX-17" could be a product code or a part number; BM25 may return multiple documents with that string in different contexts.  
  *Mitigation*: Use document structure (title vs. body field weighting) to prioritize FAQ titles; if ambiguity persists, present top-3 results.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document FAQ system, implement a lightweight hybrid system with rule-based routing. BM25 with positional phrase support handles exact codes, identifiers, and quoted phrases. Dense retrieval (using a small, general-purpose sentence encoder) handles paraphrases and natural language queries. Use RRF fusion for ambiguous queries. This balances the precision of lexical matching for known-item queries with the recall of semantic search for exploratory or variably phrased questions, without requiring GPU infrastructure at query time (dense encoding of 500 docs is a one-time offline cost; query encoding is lightweight). The routing policy keeps latency low by avoiding unnecessary dense searches for clear lexical queries. Regular qualitative evaluation on a curated test set will guide tuning as the FAQ corpus evolves.

[Proposal 3]
1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that scores documents based on term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, counting exact token matches in the document. The scoring formula is:
score(D,Q) = Σ IDF(qi) * (f(qi,D) * (k1+1)) / (f(qi,D) + k1*(1-b+b*(|D|/avgdl)))
where f(qi,D) is term frequency, |D| is document length, avgdl is average document length, and k1, b are tunable parameters. BM25 has no inherent understanding of word order, semantics, or synonyms—it only matches exact tokens after text preprocessing (tokenization, lowercasing, stemming). Positional exact-phrase matching is a separate post-retrieval or query-time operation that checks whether terms appear consecutively in the specified order; BM25 scoring alone does not enforce or reward adjacency.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a neural model (e.g., a sentence transformer). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query embedding and document embeddings in a high-dimensional vector space. The model is typically trained on paired query-document data to capture semantic similarity, paraphrasing, and topical relatedness. Retrieval is performed via approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search over pre-computed document embeddings. Dense retrieval captures synonyms and conceptual matches but may struggle with exact term matching, rare identifiers, and precise phrase constraints unless the training data specifically reinforces those patterns.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical | Dense Embedding |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap; no semantics | Semantic similarity; fuzzy token matching |
| Synonyms | No (unless query expanded) | Yes, if captured in training |
| Exact terms & identifiers | Strong (exact string match) | Weak; may map to similar but wrong terms |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires separate positional filter; BM25 score alone ignores order | No inherent phrase awareness; embedding collapses word order |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Inverted index; minimal compute; no GPU | Vector index (ANN); GPU for encoding; higher storage |
| Updates | Fast: re-index changed documents only | Requires re-encoding and index update; slower |
| Latency | Low (inverted index lookup) | Moderate (ANN search + encoding latency) |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch; misses paraphrases; no semantic generalization | Misses rare codes/IDs; false semantic matches; phrase blindness |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage approach balances latency and accuracy:
- Stage 1 (Routing): Classify query as lexical-heavy or semantic-heavy using simple heuristics:
  - If query contains alphanumeric codes (regex: \b[A-Z]+-\d+\b), quoted phrases, or is short (<4 words) with rare terms → BM25-first.
  - If query is a natural language question (>5 words), contains common verbs (forgot, cannot, how can I), or has no identifiers → Dense-first.
  - Otherwise → Hybrid (both paths).
- Stage 2 (Fusion): For hybrid queries, retrieve top-20 from BM25 and top-20 from dense. Combine scores using reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) with k=60: score(d) = Σ 1/(k+rank_i(d)). Return top-10 fused results.
- For routed queries, retrieve top-20 from primary method, then optionally re-rank top-10 using the secondary method's scores as a tiebreaker or confidence check.
- Exact-phrase queries: apply a post-retrieval positional filter on BM25 candidate documents before fusion.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations
- Risk 1: Identifier confusion in dense retrieval. Dense embeddings may map "ERR-1042" close to "ERR-1043" or "ERR-1042a" due to substring similarity in training.
  - Mitigation: Pre-process queries to detect error codes; force BM25-first route and boost exact string matches. Add a character n-gram index for fuzzy code matching as fallback.
- Risk 2: Phrase blindness in dense retrieval. Query "data export completed" may retrieve documents about "data import failed" because embeddings capture topic but not word order.
  - Mitigation: For quoted queries, apply positional phrase filter on BM25 results and suppress dense results that lack the consecutive token sequence. Use shingle-based indexing for common phrases.
- Risk 3: Vocabulary gap in BM25 for paraphrased queries. "Move workspace information" vs. document "Transfer your workspace data" yields zero BM25 overlap.
  - Mitigation: Dense-first routing catches these; for hybrid, ensure dense results are not overruled by BM25 zeros. Optionally, apply lightweight query expansion on BM25 side using FAQ-specific synonyms (move→transfer, information→data).
- Risk 4: Fusion dilution. RRF may elevate a document ranked 15th in both lists over one ranked 1st in BM25 and 50th in dense, losing a highly precise lexical match.
  - Mitigation: Weighted RRF where BM25 rank gets higher weight for code-heavy queries, or apply a minimum BM25 score threshold before fusion.

  - Semantic drift (Dense retrieved topically related but incorrect doc)

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document FAQ system, implement a lightweight hybrid with rule-based routing. BM25 alone will fail on paraphrased natural language questions (common in FAQ). Dense alone will fail on error codes, product IDs, and exact-phrase searches. A full hybrid with RRF fusion adds minimal latency for this corpus size and covers both failure modes. The routing heuristics prevent dense retrieval from mishandling identifier queries and prevent BM25 from missing semantic variations. Given the small corpus, both inverted and vector indexes can be held in memory, keeping latency under 50ms. No external vendors or GPU clusters are required; open-source dense models (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) and BM25 libraries (e.g., rank_bm25) suffice. Prioritize building the judgment set early to validate routing rules before deployment.

- Risk 3: Fusion weight mis-calibration. A fixed α may over-prioritize one signal for edge-case queries. final_output:
1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that scores documents based on term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, matching exact character sequences after tokenization. It has no inherent understanding of word meaning, synonyms, or word order beyond the presence/absence of terms. BM25 scoring itself is not positional exact-phrase matching; phrase handling requires a separate post-filter or index structure (e.g., positional index) to verify term adjacency and order.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a trained neural model (e.g., a dual-encoder). Relevance is computed as the cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query embedding and document embeddings. It captures semantic similarity, synonymy, and paraphrasing, but may struggle with exact term matching, rare identifiers, and precise phrase constraints unless the encoder was specifically fine-tuned for those patterns.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical | Dense Embedding | Hybrid |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|--------|
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap; no semantics | Semantic similarity; fuzzy token matching | Combines exact and semantic signals |
| Synonyms | No native support; requires query expansion | Captures synonyms if present in training data | Both signals available |
| Exact terms & identifiers | Strong; matches literal strings (e.g., error codes, product codes) | Weak unless fine-tuned; may map to similar but incorrect concepts | BM25 anchors exact matches; dense adds recall |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires positional index or post-filter; BM25 score alone does not enforce phrase order | No native phrase enforcement; embedding loses word order | Phrase enforcement must be layered on top of either method |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Inverted index; lightweight; no GPU | Vector index (e.g., FAISS); requires embedding model; GPU recommended for encoding | Both indexes; fusion logic; higher operational complexity |
| Updates | Add/remove documents by updating inverted index; immediate | Re-encode new documents; re-index vectors; may require incremental index rebuild | Both update paths must be maintained |
| Latency | Low; sparse dot product or term-at-a-time scoring | Moderate; nearest-neighbor search over dense vectors | Sum of both retrieval latencies plus fusion overhead |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch; misses paraphrases; over-prioritizes rare terms; no semantic generalization | Misses rare codes/identifiers; false semantic matches; embedding drift over time | Fusion weight tuning; one weak signal can dominate; increased complexity |

4. Eight-query routing table

| Query ID | Important Signal | Preferred Route | Rationale | Failure Risk |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|-----------|--------------|
| Q1. ERR-1042 reset token expired | Exact error code “ERR-1042” | BM25-first | BM25 reliably matches the literal code; dense may confuse “ERR-1042” with other error patterns | If FAQ uses variant “error 1042” without “ERR-”, BM25 may miss; mitigation: normalize error codes in index |
| Q2. I forgot my password and cannot get back into my account | Paraphrase of “password reset”/“account recovery” | Dense-first | High semantic variation; no fixed identifier; dense captures “forgot password” ≈ “reset password” | Dense may retrieve unrelated account issues; mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “password” term to anchor |
| Q3. What does product code AX-17 mean? | Exact product code “AX-17” | BM25-first | Literal string match critical; dense may not distinguish “AX-17” from “AX-18” or similar codes | If code appears only in body text without “product code” label, BM25 may rank low; mitigation: boost title/exact-match fields |
| Q4. Please send my billing receipt again | Compound concept “billing receipt”; action paraphrase “send again” | Hybrid | “billing receipt” is a specific compound; BM25 matches the terms; dense captures “send again” ≈ “resend” | Dense may retrieve general billing FAQs; mitigation: use BM25 phrase proximity on “billing receipt” |
| Q5. Disable two-factor authentication for a contractor | Specific action “disable 2FA”; entity “contractor” | Hybrid | “two-factor authentication” has synonyms (2FA, MFA); BM25 matches literal; dense handles “disable” ≈ “turn off” | Dense may conflate “contractor” with “employee”; mitigation: entity filtering if metadata available |
| Q6. I am not receiving notification emails | Symptom description; no fixed code | Dense-first | High paraphrase variability (“not getting emails”, “emails not arriving”); dense generalizes well | Dense may retrieve unrelated email configuration issues; mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “notification” term |
| Q7. Search for the exact phrase "data export completed" | Explicit exact-phrase requirement | BM25-first with phrase post-filter | Only BM25 with positional index can enforce exact phrase adjacency; dense cannot guarantee word order | If phrase is split across fields, positional check may fail; mitigation: concatenate title+body for phrase index |
| Q8. How can I move my workspace information to another account? | Semantic concept “migrate workspace data” | Dense-first | No fixed identifier; “move” ≈ “transfer” ≈ “migrate”; “workspace information” ≈ “workspace data” | Dense may retrieve “share workspace” instead of “transfer”; mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “workspace” and “account” |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document corpus, a two-stage policy balances precision and recall without heavy infrastructure:
- Stage 1 – Route: Classify query as BM25-first if it contains a known pattern (error code regex like `[A-Z]+-\d+`, product code, quoted phrase). Otherwise, route as Dense-first.
- Stage 2 – Fusion: Always retrieve top-K from both methods (K=20 each). Combine using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60: `score(d) = Σ 1/(k + rank_i(d))`. For BM25-first queries, apply a weighted RRF variant where BM25 rank receives 1.5× weight to preserve exact-match precision. For Dense-first queries, use uniform RRF weights.
- Phrase enforcement: For queries containing quoted phrases, apply a positional post-filter to BM25 candidates before fusion; documents failing the phrase check are removed. If no documents pass, fall back to dense-only results with a lower confidence flag.
- Fallback: If the top fused result has an RRF score below a tuned threshold (e.g., derived from evaluation), re-rank using a linear combination with α=0.5 as a tiebreaker and return the better list.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations
- Risk 1: Vocabulary mismatch on error codes. If FAQ uses “Error 1042” but query says “ERR-1042”, BM25 may miss. Mitigation: Normalize error codes in both index and query (e.g., strip “ERR-” prefix, case-fold) before tokenization; maintain a synonym list for known code variants.
- Risk 2: Dense embedding drift after FAQ updates. Adding new documents changes the vector space; previously good matches may degrade. Mitigation: Re-encode and re-index the full corpus on each update; for 500 docs this is cheap and fast.
- Risk 3: Fusion dilution in RRF. A document ranked 15th in both lists can outrank one ranked 1st in BM25 and 50th in dense, losing a highly precise lexical match. Mitigation: Use weighted RRF for BM25-first queries; apply a minimum BM25 score threshold before fusion for code-heavy queries.
- Risk 4: Exact-phrase false negatives. Positional post-filter may reject a document where the phrase spans a field boundary (title ends with “data export”, body starts with “completed”). Mitigation: Index a concatenated full-text field for phrase search; allow a slop of 0–1 tokens as a configurable fallback.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
- Create a judgment set: For each of the 8 query cases, manually annotate all 500 documents as relevant (1) or not (0). Include at least 3 paraphrased variants per query to test robustness (24 total query-document pairs to judge exhaustively).
- Metrics: Compute Precision@5, Recall@5, and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) for each route (BM25-only, Dense-only, Hybrid) on the annotated set.
- Error analysis: For each query, inspect false positives and false negatives. Categorize failures as: vocabulary mismatch, semantic drift, phrase violation, identifier confusion, or fusion weight error.
- Ablation: Run hybrid with RRF k ∈ {20, 40, 60, 80} and weighted vs. uniform RRF; compare MRR to validate fusion parameters.
- Phrase integrity check: For Q7, manually verify that all top-5 results contain the exact contiguous phrase “data export completed.”
- Routing accuracy: Measure whether the routing decision (BM25-first, Dense-first) would have selected the best-performing method for each query variant.
- No automated metrics requiring external models; all judgments are human-annotated.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
Deploy a hybrid system with BM25 (using an inverted index with positional information) and a dense retriever (using a lightweight dual-encoder fine-tuned on in-domain FAQ pairs if available, otherwise a general sentence encoder). Use the RRF-based routing/fusion policy described in Section 5. For 500 documents, infrastructure overhead is minimal: both indexes fit in memory, and re-indexing on updates is fast. This approach preserves exact matching for codes and phrases while adding semantic recall for paraphrased queries, directly addressing the failure modes observed in the eight query cases.
